text: The surgeon inserts a 5mm port in the epigastric region and creates pneumoperitoneum through Palmer's point. The laparoscopic view reveals grossly dilated proximal bowel loops and collapsed distal ileal loops. Adhesions are visible between the small bowel and the anterior abdominal wall, likely at the site of previous specimen extraction. The surgeon begins adhesiolysis of the small bowel adhered to the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using scissors and an atraumatic bowel grasper, gently pulling the bowel downward to reveal the dissection plane. The adjacent peritoneum is carefully dissected along with the bowel to prevent injury, with minor bleeding observed that is expected to self-resolve.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on a loop of bowel adhered to the previous operative scar, carefully dissecting away from the bowel surface and incorporating adjacent peritoneum to prevent injury. Small bleedings occur but are not troublesome.

The surgeon inspects the surface of the bowel for hemostasis and any inadvertent tears, repairing them if found. Moving distally, a thick fibrous band is observed holding the anti-mesenteric border of the terminal ileal loop, extending underneath and potentially connecting to another loop of small bowel. The band, which is avascular or minimally vascular, is divided using a harmonic shear.

The surgeon begins tracing the bowel from the ileocecal junction, observing the collapsed loops of bowel. The bowel loops appear healthy as the surgeon gently manipulates them, ensuring to hold the mesentery rather than the bowel wall if there is any doubt about the bowel's integrity.

The surgeon uses two atraumatic bowel graspers to gently pull and trace the bowel, revealing visible peristalsis. The previously hidden proximal part of a fibrous band is now visible after division, connecting the distal jejunum to the terminal ileum, which was causing obstruction.

The surgeon traces the proximal dilated bowel loops, ensuring clarity in the surgical field. Visible are two previous operative port sites, one for appendectomy and another for an unspecified procedure.
